extra.reasoning: The property requires that in every reachable state, opted_out=true implies sent=false; equivalently, no reachable state has opted_out=true AND sent=true. Transition t5 ("on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks >= 3): sent := true") directly sets sent=true while opted_out is true, so the property holds only if no reachable state satisfies (opted_out AND ticks>=3) at the moment a send_attempt occurs. That conjunction is reachable: optout sets opted_out=true (t1, guard true) and leaves ticks unchanged; tick (t4, guard true) always fires regardless of opted_out and increments ticks up to a cap of 3. Trace from the initial state (opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=0): (1) optout -> opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0; (2) tick -> ticks=1, sent=false; (3) tick -> ticks=2, sent=false; (4) tick -> ticks=3, sent=false; (5) send_attempt -> here opted_out=true and ticks=3>=3, so t5 is enabled and fires, setting sent:=true. Resulting state: opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=3, which violates globally(opted_out implies not sent). This is 5 events, within the 7-event bound. Note that at step 5 both t3 (sent:=false) and t5 (sent:=true) are enabled on send_attempt; since the semantics permit any enabled transition to fire and t5 is genuinely enabled, an execution firing t5 exists and constitutes a real violation (t5 is an opt-out bypass once ticks reaches 3). Other sent:=true source (t2) is guarded by not opted_out and is therefore safe.
